front: Work: examines four intellectuals under pseudonyms Alpha (the Moralist), Beta (the Disappointed Lover), Gamma, and Delta (the Troubadour)
Work: outlines seven forms of 'ketman' -- how dissidents deceitfully accept communism in public
Work: uses 'Murti Bing pills' from Witkiewicz's Insatiability as a metaphor for dogmatic thought
Work: 'Beta' represents Tadeusz Borowski, whose Auschwitz stories are quoted in the chapter 'Beta, the Disappointed Lover'
Poem: closes with the repeated line 'There will be no other end of the world'; those expecting 'lightning and thunder' are disappointed
back: A Song on the End of the World (Czeslaw Milosz)
work: A Song on the End of the World

front: Poem: a 'white-haired old man' who 'binds his tomatoes' is the only one who believes the title event 'is happening now'
back: A Song on the End of the World (Czeslaw Milosz)
work: A Song on the End of the World

front: Poem: a fisherman mends 'a glimmering net' and a bee circles a clover; a 'drunkard grows sleepy at the edge of a lawn'
back: A Song on the End of the World (Czeslaw Milosz)
work: A Song on the End of the World

front: Poem: four books surveying Polish literature and destruction, culminating in the Warsaw Uprising; last section set in Pennsylvania
Poem: 'poems should be written rarely and reluctantly, under unbearable duress'; 'I have always aspired to a more spacious form'
Poem: begins 'In Rome on the Campo dei Fiori / baskets of olives and lemons'
Poem: 'Do not feel safe. The poet remembers. / You can kill one, but another is born'
Poem: opens 'The history of my stupidity would fill many volumes'
Poem: ekphrastic poem on Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights, conjecturing 'mankind exists / To provision and populate Hell'
Author: frequent collaborator Robert Hass translated his work; both were at UC Berkeley
Author: early mentorship by cousin Oscar led to 'catastrophist' outlook; led the Zagary group
Author: edited the international poetry anthology A Book of Luminous Things
Author: Seamus Heaney was heavily influenced by this poet and read a poem at his funeral
